{"id": "img_82", "source_md": "Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Seasonal spatial distribution of the LAI in the model domain.", "supplement": "(a).spring;(b).summer;(c).autumn;(d).winter", "references": ["MEGAN v2.1 (Guenther et al. 2012) was utilized to estimate BVOC emissions in China with a  $27\mathrm{-km}$  horizontal grid spacing domain (shown in Fig. 1). The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "Fig. S1 and S2 show the seasonal spatial distribution of temperature at  $2\mathrm{m}$  (T2) and the daily downward shortwave radiation (DSW) simulated by the WRF model. And these meteorological factors were evaluated using in situ measurement data from 824 and 84 national meteorological sites, respectively. The in situ measurement data are from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://data.cma.cn/). Table 2 presents the verification statistics for the average daily T2 and DSW among all sites. As shown in Table 2, the mean error (ME), mean bias (MB), correlation coefficient (r) and root mean square error (RMSE) of station-averaged hourly T2 series are 2.47, -1.26, 0.95 and  $2.59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The simulation yields cooling biases of  $-0.98$ ,  $-0.90$ ,  $-1.70$  and  $-1.18^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. The ME, MB, r and RMSE values for the annual DSW series are 36.00,  $-29.88$ , 0.80 and  $81.33\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ , and the DSW simulation yields underestimations of 32.12, 43.61, 24.25 and  $19.38\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. It is because that the WRF model overestimate the cloud coverage which lead to the slightly underestimation on T2 and DSW (Wang et al. 2010; Wen et al. 2014). Compared with other studies on yearly long WRF simulation in China (Wang et al., 2014; Ying et al. 2014; Zhang et al., 2012), these biases are", "Fig. 3. Seasonal distributions of the proportions of PFTs in the model domain.", "Fig. 7a-c shows the simulated and observed daily 1-h maximum  $\mathrm{O_3}$ $(\mathrm{DM1O_3})$  concentration over China in July 2017. In general, the model successfully captured the spatial pattern over the simulated domain with highest  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  areas centralized in the east, especially in the BTH, PRD, YRD and SCB regions due to the relatively large  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and VOC emission and favorable meteorological conditions for ozone formation in these areas (e.g., little rain and strong solar radiation). And model", "It should be noted that the simulated SOA concentrations in this study have not been compared with direct measurements of organic particulate matter and SOA because of the data limitations. Extensive measurements of organic components have been conducted in megacities and major clusters in China by aerosol mass spectrometers (AMS), which provides details about various organic aerosols (Hu et al. 2016; Yang et al., 2016). And these data can be used as model validation for future local scale SOA modeling studies which can promote the understanding of SOA formation. Besides, a great deal of additional research is needed to further address the remaining questions. For example, to better quantify the separate and synergistic effects of anthropogenic and VOC emissions based on sensitivity testing, it is necessary to investigate the spatial distribution of ozone changes by comparing NOx and VOC emissions. Both chamber studies and field measurements have shown that HOMs (highly oxygenated molecules) can substantially increase the SOA mass because of their low volatility (Ehn et al. 2014). This mechanism has already been incorporated into box models (Öström et al. 2017) with detailed chemistry, and it would be interesting to observe the impact of this mechanism on SOA formation from BVOCs at the regional scale.", "Nat. content.title: Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation.
content.insight: The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.

{"id": "img_61", "source_md": "Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.", "references": ["Figure 1 shows the methodology framework in this study, including the input database and processes to fuse multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plant emissions.", "In this work, we develop a framework that fuses plant-level daily profiles from CEMS with yearly emission estimates from CPED to obtain daily emissions (Figure 1). According to guideline HJ/T 75-2017, the maximum volume fraction of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the combustion of specific fuels remains stable, so we use daily profiles of flue gas volume that takes the oxygen content into account to describe daily variations in  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions. Daily profiles of emission rates from CEMS are used for major air pollutants of  $\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{NO}_{x},$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ .", "# Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China Based on Multisource Data Fusion", "ABSTRACT: Daily emission estimates are essential for tracking the dynamic changes in emission sources. In this work, we estimate daily emissions of coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 by combining information from the unit-based China coal-fired Power plant Emissions Database (CPED) and real-time measurements from continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS). We develop a step-by-step method to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS. Then, plant-level daily profiles of flue gas volume and emissions obtained from CEMS are coupled with annual emissions from CPED to derive daily emissions. Reasonable agreement is found between emission variations and available statistics (i.e., monthly power generation and daily coal consumption). Daily power emissions are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{x}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, with high emissions in winter and summer caused", "is used to quantify monthly variations in power emissions in a unit-level power plant emission inventory in China.17 Indicator data such as power load curves are employed to indicate daily or hourly dynamics of power emissions.1,18 Recent studies have also attempted to estimate dynamically updated daily power emissions,19-22 which are largely stimulated by the demand for quantifying the evolution of emissions during fast-evolving events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic). Liu et al.19,20 used daily coal consumption from six major power generation groups in China to estimate daily updated power emissions. However, the daily coal consumption data only covers 62 power plants in China, and the activity indicator-based method is relatively indirect and lacks independent evaluations against observations. Studies utilizing satellite observations to estimate daily emission changes are faced with the challenges of differentiating power emissions from the total21,23 or deriving daily power emissions on continuous time scales.24,25 For example, Ding et al.23 used the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission reductions derived from", "In this work, taking advantage of a well-validated unit-based power emission database covering China and the high temporal resolution of CEMS data, we attempt to construct daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China based on multisource data fusion. Careful screening of outliers and imputation of missing values are conducted for the flue gas volume and emission data. Then, the plant-level daily profiles (i.e., coefficients for day-to-day variations in a year) from CEMS are combined with the unit-level annual emissions from", "2.1.1. Unit-Level Emission Database of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China. The CPED, $^{17}$  developed by Tsinghua University, is a high-resolution emission inventory that provides year-by-year emissions from coal-fired power plants since 1990 in China. Established based on detailed and unit-level information from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, undisclosed data), CPED contains unit locations, operational status, unit capacity, coal consumption, control technologies, and emission factors, which enable accurate spatial allocation and emission estimates for power plants. More details about CPED can be found in the paper by Liu et al. $^{16}$  Previous studies have verified that the magnitude and trends of power emissions in CPED are in good agreement with top-down estimates from satellite measurements. $^{43,50}$  Liu et al. $^{43}$  compared  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in CPED with those based on satellite data and found rather good agreement with the relative differences of  $4 \pm 18\%$ . CPED used here covers 30 provinces (except Hong Kong, Macao, Taiwan, and Tibet), 3948 power plants, and 8722 in-use electricity-generating units from 2017 to 2020.", "In this study, data from CPED and CEMS are fused to improve the daily estimation of emissions from coal-fired power units in China. The comprehensive, highly resolved emission database could capture the dynamic changes in emissions during typical socioeconomic events and could be applied to providing more accurate inputs for CTMs and guiding future policies.", "In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired", "In this work, we estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 through data fusion of a well-validated unit-based power emission database and high-temporal-resolution measurements from CEMS. A step-by-step method is developed to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS, and sensitivity tests show that monthly and weekly temporal profiles by different imputation methods are in good agreement ( $R = 0.90 - 0.98$  for national scale,  $R = 0.61 - 0.92$  for provincial level). We find that the temporal profiles from CEMS agree well with available statistical data like monthly power generation and daily coal consumption, with  $R$  greater than 0.7 for 16-27 out of 30 provinces at the monthly scale and  $R$  up to 0.83 at the daily scale. Daily power emissions from the fused database are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, during 2017-2020, reflecting higher emissions in winter and summer caused by heating and cooling demand and lower ones resulting from holidays. Our estimates are also able to capture the sudden changes in daily power emissions during typical socioeconomic events, including the much slower rebound of power emissions after the Spring Festival holiday due to the impact of COVID-19, higher daily  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from thermal power generation in Yunnan owing to a severe drought, and significant decline of power emissions in Wuhan caused by a short-term emission control during the 7th CISM Military World Games. We also find that previous studies (e.g.,  $\mathrm{EDGAR}^1$ ) that apply fixed parameters to allocate weekly power emission totals to daily scales exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. This work provides a framework to propagate information from real-time emission measurements to high-temporal-resolution emission estimates, which will support dynamic emission tracking and air pollution modeling.", "(6) Nassar, R.; Napier-Linton, L.; Gurney, K. content.title: Figure 4. Effects of a drought on  CO_{2}  emissions from thermal power plants in Yunnan Province are presented in panels (a) and (b). The impact of emission control measures on  SO_{2}  emissions during the 7th CISM Military World Games is shown in (c) and (d). (a) Relative changes in  CO_{2}  emissions in Yunnan Province and other provinces from 2019 to 2020. (b) Relative changes in hydropower generation and thermal power generation in Yunnan Province from 2019 to 2020. Since January and February are usually combined in the statistical data released by the National Bureau of Statistics, they are plotted as a whole here. (c) Daily  SO_{2}  emissions before, during, and after the emission control period in Wuhan, other cities in Hubei, and other provinces except Hubei. The light gray shading covers the periods of short-term emission control. The daily emissions are normalized to the value on October 6. (d) Relative changes in average  SO_{2}  emissions during the emission control period compared to the periods before and after this period.
content.summary: The figure depicts the impact of drought on CO2 emissions and power generation types in Yunnan, and SO2 emissions during the CISM Military World Games in Wuhan and surrounding areas.
content.insight: The figure illustrates notable changes in emission patterns due to environmental factors and control measures, highlighting differences in CO2 and SO2 emissions across regions and timeframes.

content.title: Figure 5. Comparisons of monthly and weekly profiles based on flue gas volume or emission rates from CEMS and those widely used in previous studies. (a) Monthly temporal profiles derived from national  SO_2  emission,  NO_x  emission,  PM_{2.5}  emission, and flue gas volume data from CEMS or electricity generation. The electricity-generation data is accessed from the National Bureau of Statistics (https://data.stats.gov.cn/). (b) Weekly temporal profiles calculated by national average flue gas volume,  SO_2  emission,  NO_x  emission, and  PM_{2.5}  emission data from CEMS or from previous studies including that of He et al. ^{57}  and the EDGAR. ^1
content.summary: Figure 5 shows monthly and weekly profiles of emissions and flue gas volume from CEMS compared with previous studies, illustrating differences in temporal patterns.
content.insight: CEMS profiles align well with electricity generation data for monthly scales but reveal discrepancies in weekly patterns, challenging previous studies' assumptions about weekday and weekend emission differences.

At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. content.title: Fig. 1. Comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District. (a) Xiaoshan District (the red area) is located in the hinterland of the YRD in China. (b) Comprehensive traffic monitoring achieves full coverage over Xiaoshan District. Each dot represents a set of comprehensive traffic monitoring facilities that can recognize license plates, traffic fluxes, vehicle-specific speed, and vehicle categories. Such road segments are divided into three road classes: highways (blue lines), arterial roads (pink lines), and residential streets (green lines). Map data © 2022, Gaode Map.

Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.", "references": ["The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\\%$  of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "$\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.", "Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. In order to find out the content of crustal elements in the", "The mass concentration of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.", "In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\\%$  and  $63.36\\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\\%$  and  $93.32\\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{F}^{-} > \\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{Na}^{+} > \\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \\mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \\mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\\%$ ,  $1.788\\%$ ,  $0.773\\%$ ,  $2.186\\%$  and  $0.623\\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\\%$ ,  $0.789\\%$ ,  $1.782\\%$ ,  $2.918\\%$  and  $0.857\\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. Thus, humidity helped increase the percentage of water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and still keep the order of top water-soluble ions. Cheng et al. (2010) studied the on-road  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Hong Kong, a coastal city with higher humidity than Wuhan. They found the percentage of", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Mg} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Zn} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\\mathrm{K} > \\mathrm{Na} > \\mathrm{Ca} > \\mathrm{Sr} > \\mathrm{Fe} > \\mathrm{Cr} > \\mathrm{Pb} > \\mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\\%$  that reported by", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\\%$  and  $51.151\\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. source_args.query_intent: 江苏省PM2.5排放因素
江苏省PM2.5排放因素

img_170.source_reason: 江苏省的PM10和PM2.5排放存在显著差异，我将通过搜索不同行业间排放因素寻找更多信息。

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图表显示随着中国排放标准升级，PM2.5排放因子显著下降。柴油车在同一标准下的PM2.5排放量高于汽油车。
evaluation.reason: 该证据提供了具体的排放因子比较以及标准升级对PM2.5排放的影响，适合进一步研究不同车辆类型的环境影响。

content.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: Figure 6 illustrates the emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles under China III, IV, and V standards.
content.insight: The figure shows higher emission factors for Na, Mg, K, and Ca, indicating significant contributions from fuel additives, with lower emissions for other elements from engine wear and catalytic particles.

{"id": "img_173", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .", "references": ["It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "# 3.1. Emission factors and rates of  $PM_{2,5}$", "# 3.4. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.", "The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . Their percentages in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased slightly with high humidity and the order of top water-soluble ions kept the same. Their emission factors of diesel vehicles were all larger than gasoline vehicles. The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. It occupied  $53.93\%$  and  $51.15\%$  in gasoline and diesel vehicles, respectively. Fig. 3 Spatial distribution of industrial heat source objects", "# An Industrial Heat Source Extraction Method: BP Neural Network Using Temperature Feature Template", "Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "图1基于BP神经网络的工业热源对象提取方法技术流程

content.title: Figure 3. Correlations between normalized provincial industrial emissions and three types of spatial proxies: (a) total population, (b) urban population, and (c) IGDP data.
content.summary: Figure 3 presents scatter plots depicting correlations between normalized provincial industrial emissions and three spatial proxies: total population, urban population, and IGDP.
content.insight: The figure highlights the varying strengths of correlation between industry emissions and different spatial proxies, suggesting that IGDP may offer a more robust proxy compared to population metrics.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_143", "source_md": "Impact of spatial proxies on the representation of.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Impact of spatial proxies on the representation of\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Figure 3. Correlations between normalized provincial industrial emissions and three types of spatial proxies: (a) total population, (b) urban population, and (c) IGDP data.", "references": ["In the last dataset (S6), further modifications of S5 are made to better represent the spatial patterns of the industrial sector. We use the industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) as a first-step spatial proxy instead of population density. Figure 3 shows the correlations between the normalized industrial emissions and three factors (total population, urban", "obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.", "For the four municipalities studied, total populations and nighttime lights alone cannot effectively represent emission patterns because these proxies are concentrated in city centers, whereas large emission sources, such as power plants and industrial activities, have largely been relocated away from urban regions in municipalities. When the locations of power emissions are considered, the correlation between the modeled and observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns is significantly improved (Fig. 5j, n, and r). However, using the IGDP and updated road network as spatial proxies disproportionately concentrates emissions in urban areas, resulting in an overestimation of modeled  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["normalized provincial industrial emissions", "total population", "urban population", "industrial GDP", "spatial proxies", "correlations"], "concise_summary": "Figure 3 presents scatter plots depicting correlations between normalized provincial industrial emissions and three spatial proxies: total population, urban population, and IGDP.", "inferred_insight": "The figure highlights the varying strengths of correlation between industry emissions and different spatial proxies, suggesting that IGDP may offer a more robust proxy compared to population metrics.", "paper_id": 21}

content.title: Figure 1. (a) Source-specific  CO_{2}  emission in each province. Each group of bars represent a region (from left to right): northwest (Xinjiang-Shaanxi), northeast (Neimenggu-Liaoning), north (Beijing-Henan), central (Hubei-Jiangxi), southwest (Xizang-Guangxi), and southeast (Jiangsu-Taiwan). Ten provinces and municipalities with emissions lower than  1000Ggyr^{-1}  were shown in detail in (b): Gansu (GS), Ningxia (NX), Qinghai (QH), Shaanxi (SX), Beijing (BJ), Tianjin (TJ), Chongqing (CQ), Shanghai (SH), Hainan (HN), and Taiwan (TW). Macao and Hong Kong have minimal emissions:  0.5Gg  in Macao, consisting of  0.4Gg  from forest fires and  0.1Gg  from grassland fires; and  24.7Gg  in Hong Kong, consisting of  20.6Gg  ( 83\% ) from forest fires,  2.7Gg  ( 11\% ) from grassland fires,  0.7Gg  from shrubland, and  0.7Gg  from cropland fires.
content.summary: The figure shows CO2 emissions by source in Chinese provinces, with notable contributions from forests, grasslands, cropland, and shrubland fires. It highlights provinces with lower emissions in detail.
content.insight: The figure emphasizes differential regional contributions to CO2 emissions, with forest fires being the largest contributor nationwide, varying regionally with agricultural and crop residue burning.

content.title: Fig.9 Number of thermal anomaly points from January to May

This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. content.title: Fig. 2. The box-and-whisker plots showing observed daily  PM_{2.5}  concentrations (left Y axis), and the histograms (right Y axis) representing the daily dry matter burned based on GFED (blue solid bars) and VIIRS (blue hatched bars) in November 2017 over three provinces, (a) Heilongjiang Province, (b) Jilin Province and (c) Liaoning Province. The box-and-whisker plots include the minimum, maximum (line end points), 25th percentile, 75th percentile (boxes), medians (black lines), and average (red points). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
content.summary: Box-and-whisker plots depict daily PM2.5 concentrations, while histograms show dry matter burned data in three provinces during November 2017, comparing GFED and VIIRS data.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the correlation between daily PM2.5 concentrations and dry matter burned across different regions, highlighting differences in GFED and VIIRS data measurements.

content.title: Fig. 7. a, mean diurnal cycle of simulated  PM_{2.5}  concentrations, downward surface shortwave radiation based on case 5, as well as their changes due to aerosol radiative effects ((case 5 - case 6) - (case 1 - case 2)) over Heilongjiang (top black box in Fig. S1b) from November 1 to 3, 2017. b, the same as a, but the meteorological parameters were changed to PBL height.
content.summary: The figure shows diurnal cycles of PM2.5 concentrations and atmospheric parameters over Heilongjiang, illustrating changes due to aerosol radiative effects over a three-day period in November 2017.

content.title: Fig. 6. As in Fig. 5 but for tropical and oceanic regions.
content.summary: Figure 6 presents methanol column densities in various tropical and oceanic regions, comparing predictions and IASI observations. Predicted values often exceed the observed data, highlighting differences across various regions such as the Pacific and Indian Ocean.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the model's methanol over-predictions compared to IASI data, particularly over oceanic regions. This mismatch indicates potential model adjustments are needed, especially regarding emission rates and seasonal variations in these regions.

Nevertheless, the model/IASI comparisons over the Pacific, Atlantic and Indian ocean do not confirm these conclusions (Fig. 6). The predicted columns turn out to be higher than IASI observations in almost all regions and months, and they exhibit almost no seasonal variation, as opposed to the observations. It should be noted, however, that the IASI signal over the oceans is weak and large errors are associated with the columns. This issue warrants further study and underscores the need for further measurements at remote oceanic sites.", "The MEGANv2.1 inventory reproduces better the magnitude and seasonal variation of the observed columns in most regions. This is also reflected in the correlation coefficients given in Figs. 5 and 6. In Indonesia, the S2 model overprediction with respect to IASI columns (factor of 2, Fig. 5) is in line with comparisons between modelled and observed methanol concentrations obtained during the OP3 campaign conducted in 2008 in the Malaysian rainforest during the wet (April-May) and early dry season (June-July) (Langford et al., 2010). More specifically, the S2 methanol concentrations calculated at the model grid cell comprising the campaign site located in northeastern Borneo  $(5^{\circ}\mathrm{N}, 118^{\circ}\mathrm{E})$  are 2.5 times higher than the concentrations of  $1.2 \pm 0.46\mathrm{ppbv}$ , and  $1.5 \pm 0.67\mathrm{ppbv}$  observed in the wet and dry season, respectively. This finding could be partly explained by an overestimation of the methanol emission rate assumed in MEGANv2.1 for broadleaf forests  $(400\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ , Sect. 2.2). In fact, flux measurements of methanol reported in Langford et al. (2010) at this rainforest site have a mean value very close to zero and a large variability  $(-33 \pm 300\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$ , suggesting that dry deposition plays an important role. Furthermore, these fluxes over Borneo are much lower than the net fluxes measured in the Amazon rainforest by Karl et al. (2004)  $(500\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$ . Such considerable discrepancies make evident that large variability is associated to measured fluxes even for relatively similar rainforest biomes, and therefore, the use of a single flux value for all broadleaf trees in MEGANv2.1 cannot be but a crude estimate. In Amazonia, the S2 model overestimation with regard to IASI reaches up to  $50 - 70\%$  depending on the region (Fig. 6), but it is much more significant in the S1 simulation, in line with the findings of Millet et al. (2008). The use of MEGANv2.1 produces a nice agreement with IASI over the Goias province in central Brazil, a region of Brazil covered with woodland savanna, and Uruguay, mainly covered with tall prairie grass plains, lending confidence to the assumed methanol rates for grasslands in MEGANv2.1 (Fig. 6).", "Annually averaged oceanic methanol emission fluxes and deposition velocity  $(= (H / k_{\mathrm{w}} + 1 / k_{\mathrm{g}})^{-1}, \mathrm{cm} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1})$ , calculated using the above parameterization are shown in Fig. 2. The global methanol oceanic source amounts to  $43 \, \mathrm{Tg}$ , in good agreement with Heikes et al. (2002)  $(30 \, \mathrm{Tg} \, \mathrm{yr}^{-1})$ , whereas the calculated uptake with the IMAGESv2 model is estimated at  $48 - 56 \, \mathrm{Tg}$  (Table 1), resulting in a global annual net methanol sink of ca.  $5 - 13 \, \mathrm{Tg} \, \mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ . This sink is largest  $(>7 \times 10^{9} \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{cm}^{-2} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1})$  near continental coasts, and becomes a weak source over open water regions, in agreement with reported results by Millet et al. (2008). However, the ocean source (and sink) estimated by Millet et al. (2008) is twice as large as our estimate. This difference stems mainly from the use of an older parameterization (Asher, 1997) for the gas-phase transfer coefficient  $k_{\mathrm{g}}$  in Millet et al. (2008). In fact, the use of Millet et al. (2008) parameterization in our model would result in an ocean source estimate of  $90 \, \mathrm{Tg} \, \mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ , very close to the Millet et al. The model concentrations have been averaged over the same regions taking into account the location of the measurements. Comparisons between INTEX-A and NEAQS data and modelled concentrations below  $1\mathrm{km}$  are shown in Fig. 8.", "cob et al. (2005) might not be adequate for tropical regions, both in terms of seasonality and magnitude. Over Europe, the S2 run results in a moderate underestimation and to a less marked seasonal cycle compared to IASI (Fig. 5).", "In the OptS2-conv inversion a factor of two increase in the convective updraft fluxes used in the model is assumed. Stronger convection leads to a redistribution of trace gases from the boundary layer upwards in the free troposphere, in regions with generally higher horizontal winds resulting in a stronger dispersion and lower methanol columns over source regions. In tropical regions, however, where horizontal winds are weak, this decrease is more than compensated by an increase in the modelled methanol columns calculated with the IASI averaging kernels, because of the higher sensitivity of the retrievals to the higher tropospheric layers. Regionally, the annual biogenic emission change inferred by OptS2-conv lies within  $10\%$  of the standard inversion (Fig. 13), whereas on the global scale the a posteriori biogenic source stays very close to that of the standard inversion. The largest emission changes are found over Amazonia, where about  $10\%$  lower emissions are required to match the IASI columns when convection is enhanced. As expected, the a posteriori column changes are very small (less than  $2\%$ ) over source regions, due to the observational constraint. Enhanced transport from source regions to remote areas leads to small methanol column increases over oceans and deserts (Fig. 13). Comparison with the INTEX-A and NEAQS vertical profiles (Fig. 14,", "Another important removal process for methanol is the oxidation by OH. By using the prescribed climatological OH fields by Spivakovsky et al. (1990), the OptS2-OH sensitivity inversion acknowledges the large uncertainties that currently exist on modelled OH concentrations, especially over tropical forests, and also in mid-latitude continental regions (Lelieveld et al., 2008; Ren et al., 2008; Hofzumahaus et al., 2009). Note that the Spivakovsky et al. (1990) parameterization ignores completely the depleting effect of isoprene and other biogenic VOC emissions on OH, and therefore might overestimate the OH concentrations over forested areas, whereas isoprene emissions strongly deplete OH levels in IMAGESv2 despite the OH regeneration included in the MIM2+ mechanism used by the model, leading to substantial OH underestimations against campaign measurements over Amazonia and in the Eastern US (Stavrakou et al., 2010). The inferred emission changes, illustrated in Figure 13, reflect directly the differences between the modelled and the prescribed OH fields. In particular, moderate changes in the biogenic emissions are derived over Amazonia, Europe and US (less than  $10\%$ ), whereas decreases of up to  $30\%$  are found over central and south Asia. The lower OH concentrations of Spivakovsky et al. (1990) compared to IMAGESv2 at northern mid-latitudes lead to increased methanol lifetime, and consequently, to lower emission fluxes required to match the observations, as also reflected in the weaker vertical gradient of methanol mixing ratios over the US (Fig. 14, red line). Methanol is also more efficiently exported from mid-latitude emitting regions strongly constrained by IASI data, to oceanic or desertic regions where the methanol columns are low and weakly constrained by the observations, as testified by the column increases shown in Fig. 13. The global plant emission is estimated to  $91.5\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , i.e. by  $8\%$  lower than in the standard case, the decrease being more significant in the extratropics (Table 3).", "plete year of global methanol column abundances retrieved from the IASI sounder has been used in order to evaluate the model in terms of magnitude and seasonal variation of methanol concentrations. The agreement between the IASI columns and the model is considerably improved when MEGANv2.1 is used, compared to the simulation using the Jacob et al. (2005) database. In the latter case, large model overestimations have been found, especially over tropical forests, in line with findings by Millet et al. (2008), whereas poorly reproduced seasonal variations in the Tropics indicate that the parameterization based on net primary productivity is not suitable in these regions. Using MEGANv2.1 as a priori inventory, we have conducted an inverse modelling experiment based on the adjoint of IMAGESv2 and constrained by IASI columns in order to improve the estimates of methanol emissions from plants and vegetation fires. The main conclusions drawn from this study are summarized below.", "- The global a posteriori biogenic methanol source deduced from the inversion amounts to  $100\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  and is very close to a priori value of  $105\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ . The methanol source from vegetation fires is also decreased by  $20\%$  globally with respect to the GFEDv3 database, but stronger decreases (up to a factor of two) are inferred over central and Southern Africa.  
- Annual reductions of the biogenic methanol source are suggested by the IASI data over Amazonia and Indonesia, by  $40 - 55\%$  and  $45 - 58\%$ , respectively. Over Amazonia, the derived source allows for a very satisfactory agreement with the satellite data, yet it leads to a model underprediction of about  $20\%$  with respect to a limited set of available surface methanol measurements. It appears plausible that the IASI columns might be biased low over tropical forests due to possible cloud contamination problems.  
- An annual decrease by  $20 - 25\%$  of the biogenic methanol source is suggested over the Eastern US, leading to a good agreement with aircraft data in the boundary layer, while the model systematically underestimates the methanol concentrations in the free troposphere over land and ocean, in line with previous modelling studies. Possible reasons for this underestimation include an underestimation in the Western US methanol source, an underestimation of convective fluxes, and/or an overestimation of OH simulated concentrations.  
- The biogenic methanol source is strongly increased with respect to MEGANv2.1 over arid and semi-arid regions of Central Asia, Western US, and Northern Australia. This might be related to an underestimation of the methanol emission rate from shrub or to an unaccounted source of methanol specific to such environments in the MEGANv2.1 model. content.title: Fig.8 Daily average number of industrial heat anomaly points in four periods of epidemic prevention and control
content.summary: The figure shows daily average industrial heat anomaly points across different phases of epidemic control in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and Wuhan from 2019 to 2021.
content.insight: The figure indicates variations in industrial heat anomalies during different epidemic control phases, highlighting differences in activity patterns across regions and years.
